cd /var/folders/bf/qrdhv26549x53hy5n0w670jc0000gn/T/sreft-cpu-regex-297240122 && find app -type f && echo '===MAIN===' && cat app/*.go 2>/dev/null | grep -n -i 'waf\|healthz\|regex\|WAF_RULE_DISABLED\|check\|MustCompile' | head -40

      WAF_RULE_DISABLED: "1"

input.cmd: sleep 3; for i in 1 2 3; do curl -sS -m 5 http://localhost:8081/healthz -w ' [http=%{http_code} time=%{time_total}]\n' 2>&1; done; echo '---STATS---'; docker stats --no-stream --format 'table {{.Name}}\t{{.CPUPerc}}' sreft-regex-svc sreft-cpu-payments

curl -sS -m 5 "http://localhost:8081/check?q=aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa" -w ' [http=%{http_code} time=%{time_total}]\n' 2>&1; echo '---STATUS---'; curl -sS -m 5 http://localhost:8081/status 2>&1; echo '---LOG---'; docker logs --tail 3 sreft-regex-svc 2>&1

cat > /Users/peteshima/workplace/sre-field-tests/runs/subscription-20260710T221828Z/cpu-regex__default__seed3__20260711T011407Z/submission.json <<'EOF'
{
  "root_cause": "regex-svc was CPU-saturated by a ReDoS (regular-expression denial of service). Its 'WAF rule' matched request input against the catastrophically-backtracking pattern ^(a+)+$ using the dlclark/regexp2 backtracking engine with NO match timeout. The load generator (sreft-cpu-load, 6 concurrent workers) sent crafted payloads (~26 'a' characters) that each took ~5 seconds of backtracking to evaluate on the container's single capped CPU. Requests are served from a fixed pool of 4 workers, and /healthz is served through that SAME pool. With all workers tied up in multi-second regex evaluations, the health probe could not obtain a worker and timed out (curl to :8081/healthz hung for the full 5s and returned nothing), while the process itself stayed alive and pinned at ~100% CPU. This was a live configuration exposure, not a crash: the vulnerable WAF rule was active because WAF_RULE_DISABLED was intentionally left unset on regex-svc, whereas the stable payments neighbor runs the same image with WAF_RULE_DISABLED=1.",
  "actions_taken": "1) Triaged with docker ps / docker stats and found sreft-regex-svc pinned at ~100% CPU while its neighbors were idle. 2) Confirmed the outage by probing http://localhost:8081/healthz, which timed out after 5s with no response. 3) Read the service logs ('WAF rule ENABLED (vulnerable), workers=4') and the app source (app/main.go), confirming the ^(a+)+$ backtracking rule, the shared 4-worker pool, and the WAF_RULE_DISABLED=1 kill switch. 4) Applied the least-risky fix (the built-in kill switch / rule rollback): added WAF_RULE_DISABLED=\"1\" to the regex-svc service environment in bootstrap/tier0/docker-compose.yaml and recreated only that service with 'docker compose up -d regex-svc'. 5) Verified recovery: /healthz returns 200 in ~14ms, /check returns 200 with {\"rule\":\"disabled\"} in ~23ms even while the load generator keeps attacking, and CPU fell from ~100% to ~2%. Did NOT touch the payments or load containers, and made no destructive changes.",
  "postmortem": "A malicious/high-volume request pattern exploited a WAF rule built on a catastrophically-backtracking regular expression (^(a+)+$) evaluated by a backtracking engine with no timeout. Because request handling used a small fixed worker pool that also served the health endpoint, a handful of slow evaluations exhausted every worker and starved health checks, making a still-running process appear down. This is a blameless systems issue, not an individual error. Remediation was to disable the vulnerable rule via its existing kill switch, immediately freeing the pool. Follow-ups to prevent recurrence: (1) enforce a per-evaluation regex match timeout / deadline (regexp2 supports MatchTimeout) so no single request can monopolize a worker; (2) avoid backtracking-prone patterns or use an RE2-style linear-time engine for untrusted input; (3) bound input length before evaluation; (4) isolate the health check from the request worker pool (dedicated goroutine/liveness path) so probes reflect process liveness independently of load; (5) add CPU-saturation and health-latency alerting and rate limiting on /check; (6) make the WAF kill switch a first-class, one-command runbook action."
}
EOF
echo "written"; python3 -c "import json;json.load(open('/Users/peteshima/workplace/sre-field-tests/runs/subscription-20260710T221828Z/cpu-regex__default__seed3__20260711T011407Z/submission.json'));print('valid json')"
